attributes.stem: A 67-year-old woman presents to her General Practitioner after an episode of right eye blindness. She describes the blindness as “like a curtain coming down over the eye”, which resolved spontaneously within minutes. She denies any other symptoms including headache or pain over her temples. She has never had a similar episode before. She is otherwise well, with no fever, history of limb weakness, difficulty with speech, or sensory changes. She has a medical history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidaemia. She takes metformin, gliclazide, and rosuvastatin. She has a 30 pack-year smoking history and drinks about six standard glasses of wine a week.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying condition?
attributes.explanation:
This is a classical description of amaurosis fugax, which is caused by transient ischaemia of the retina. One of the common causes is an embolic event in the retinal or ophthalmic artery, which is a form of transient ischaemic attack (TIA). The embolus typically originates from dislodgement of material from a plaque within the internal carotid artery. Further investigation and treatment is necessary due to the risk of recurrent TIA or ischaemic stroke.

Although migraines may occasionally present with visual blurring or loss, it would be unusual for a migraine to present so late in life and with no prior history. The resolution within minutes is also not consistent with migraine. Additionally, migraines typically present with headache, photophobia, phonophobia, and preceding aura, all of which are absent in this patient.

It would be unusual for vertebrobasilar insufficiency to present with isolated visual symptoms. More typically, patients would describe a constellation of non-specific symptoms which may include vertigo, bilateral paraesthesia or ataxia, dysphasia, and diplopia. While insufficiency of the posterior cerebral circulation can cause occipital lobe ischaemia and therefore visual symptoms, the specific description of monocular blindness suggests a pathology anterior to the optic chiasm.

Temporal arteritis (or giant cell arteritis) classically presents with headache, transient monocular vision loss, and jaw claudication in a patient with constitutional signs or fever. It is a diagnosis which must be excluded in this patient, however without any associated symptoms it is more likely that this is an embolic event.

Multiple sclerosis (MS) may present as monocular vision loss, however it is more common in younger patients. Furthermore, MS is characterised by various neurological deficits at different times, which is not consistent with this history.
learningPoints: Symptomatic carotid artery stenosis commonly presents with transient visual changes.
references: Feroze KB, O'Rourke MC. Transient Loss Of Vision. [Updated 2020 Jul 21]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2020 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430845/
